papers.title: Evaluating AI-Generated Molecules for Drug Discovery: From Generic Metrics to Translational Readiness.
papers.abstract: Artificial intelligence-driven molecular generation has become an increasingly used computational approach for proposing candidate chemical structures in early-stage drug discovery, yet the practical value of the molecules produced is often difficult to judge. Many studies still rely mainly on model-level metrics such as validity, uniqueness, novelty, and diversity. These metrics describe whether a generator produces parsable, non-redundant structures that extend beyond a reference set, but they do not show whether the molecules are chemically credible, biologically relevant, or experimentally
papers.summary: This paper argues that current evaluation metrics for AI-generated molecules in drug discovery (like validity, uniqueness, novelty, and diversity) are insufficient because they don't assess chemical credibility, biological relevance, or experimental translatability. The authors advocate for more practical, translational readiness metrics to better judge the real-world value of AI-designed drug candidates.

papers.title: Exploration and experimental validation of oxidative stress-related diagnostic genes in interstitial cystitis based on transcriptomics.
papers.abstract: Interstitial cystitis (IC) is a chronic pain syndrome with an elusive diagnosis and poorly understood pathogenesis, in which oxidative stress (OS) is increasingly implicated. This study aimed to identify and validate OS-related diagnostic biomarkers for IC using an integrative computational and experimental approach.
papers.summary: This study identified and validated oxidative stress-related genes as potential diagnostic biomarkers for interstitial cystitis by combining transcriptomic analysis with experimental confirmation. The findings provide new insights into the role of oxidative stress in this chronic pain syndrome and offer candidate biomarkers for improved diagnosis.

papers.title: Artificial Intelligence in Inherited Epidermolysis Bullosa: Current Evidence, Challenges, and Future Directions.
papers.abstract: Epidermolysis bullosa (EB) comprises a group of rare inherited genodermatoses characterized by fragility and blistering of the skin and mucous membranes, chronic wounding, and significant morbidity including increased risk of squamous cell carcinoma in severe subtypes. Key unmet priorities include reducing diagnostic latency, establishing objective wound monitoring, enabling early detection of malignant transformation within chronic ulcerations, and developing therapies that durably modify disease progression. Artificial intelligence (AI) encompassing machine learning (ML), and deep learning (
papers.summary: This review examines how artificial intelligence (including machine learning and deep learning) could help address key challenges in epidermolysis bullosa, such as faster diagnosis, objective wound monitoring, early cancer detection, and development of disease-modifying therapies. The authors discuss current evidence and future directions for AI applications in this rare genetic skin disorder.

papers.title: In Silico Prioritisation of Similarity-Selected Small Molecules Targeting the IsdB NEAT Domain of Staphylococcus aureus as a Potential Antivirulence Strategy.
papers.abstract: The increasing prevalence of multidrug-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) has necessitated the development of alternative therapeutic strategies targeting bacterial virulence factors. This study employed an integrated in silico approach to identifying potential inhibitors of the iron-regulated surface determinant B Near-iron Transporter domain, a key protein involved in heme acquisition and pathogenicity. Virtual screening and molecular docking identified certain similarity-selected small molecules possessing strong binding affinities, with (4-(1-oxoisoindolin-2-yl)benzoic acid (TOP1) and
papers.summary: This study used computer-based methods to identify small molecules that could block a key protein (IsdB NEAT domain) used by MRSA to acquire iron and cause disease. Two compounds, including TOP1, showed strong potential binding, offering a possible new antivirulence strategy against drug-resistant S. aureus.

papers.title: Integrated Screening Cascades for Ion-Channel Drug Discovery: Linking Structure, Electrophysiology, Safety Pharmacology, and Human-Relevant Models.
papers.abstract: Ion channels are validated drug targets, but they remain difficult to study as their pharmacology is influenced by rapid gating, conformational state transitions, cell-type-specific expression, and narrow safety margins. Recent advances in cryo-electron microscopy, structure-based in silico screening, machine-learning-guided prioritization, optical high-throughput screening, automated patch-clamp electrophysiology, and human-relevant organoid or microphysiological system (MPS) models are transforming this field. In this expanded review, we examine how these modalities can be integrated into a
papers.summary: This review discusses how recent advances in cryo-electron microscopy, machine learning, automated patch-clamp, and human organoid models can be integrated into screening cascades to improve ion-channel drug discovery, addressing challenges like rapid gating and safety margins. It provides a framework for linking structural, electrophysiological, and human-relevant data to streamline the development of safer and more effective ion-channel-targeting drugs.

papers.title: In Silico Design of Pyrimidine Derivatives as Potential α-Glucosidase Inhibitors: QSAR, Molecular Docking, ADMET, and Molecular Dynamics Studies.
papers.abstract: Diabetes mellitus remains a major metabolic disorder requiring the development of new and effective α-glucosidase inhibitors. The present study aimed to identify, design, and optimize novel 3-amino-2,4-diarylbenzo[4,5]imidazo[1,2-α]pyrimidine derivatives with promising inhibitory activity against the α-glucosidase enzyme using a comprehensive in silico strategy. Approximately 300 molecular descriptors were calculated to characterize a dataset of 32 compounds (Peytam et al.) and to investigate the structural factors governing their biological activity. Based on these descriptors, a multiple lin
papers.summary: This study used computational methods to design new pyrimidine-based compounds that could inhibit α-glucosidase, a target for diabetes treatment. By analyzing molecular features and simulating how these compounds interact with the enzyme, the researchers identified promising drug candidates. The work combines QSAR, docking, and molecular dynamics to guide the development of potential anti-diabetic agents.

papers.title: Harnessing data science to control non-communicable diseases in Africa: a systematic review and gap analysis.
papers.abstract: Data science methods can provide novel and pragmatic approaches for preventing and controlling non-communicable diseases (NCDs) in Africa. This study highlights current efforts, opportunities, and challenges in leveraging data science methods to accelerate and advance the prevention and control of NCDs in Africa.
papers.summary: This systematic review examines how data science methods can be used to prevent and control non-communicable diseases (NCDs) in Africa, highlighting current efforts, opportunities, and challenges. The study identifies gaps in the application of data science for NCD management in the African context.

papers.title: Dynamics-enhanced molecular property prediction guided by deep learning.
papers.abstract: Molecular property prediction (MPP) is a key challenge in computational biology and drug discovery. Traditional approaches mostly rely on feature representations yielded from static structures of molecules, ignoring their dynamic nature. The scarcity of dynamics data in public databases and the complexity of learning such high-dimensional data made it more difficult for dynamics-involved studies. Accordingly, we built a series of dynamics datasets for MPP tasks by performing comprehensive molecular dynamics (MD) simulations on different molecules. In addition, we proposed a dynamically enhance
papers.summary: This study addresses the challenge of molecular property prediction by incorporating molecular dynamics data, which captures the dynamic behavior of molecules rather than relying solely on static structures. The authors created new dynamics datasets through extensive molecular dynamics simulations and developed a deep learning approach to leverage this dynamic information for improved predictions. This work provides both a valuable resource and a methodological advance for computational biology and drug discovery.

papers.title: Integration of molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, and free energy calculations identifies a novel flavonoid glycoside as a PCSK9 inhibitor for hypercholesterolemia.
papers.abstract: Proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9 (PCSK9) significantly influences plasma low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) homeostasis by promoting the degradation of LDL receptors. While monoclonal antibody therapies are effective, their widespread application is limited by high costs and invasive administration, necessitating the development of oral small-molecule inhibitors for hypercholesterolemia management. This study evaluates the therapeutic potential of a specific flavonoid glycoside, 7-(alpha-D-Glucopyranosyloxy)-5-hydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-4 H-1-benzopyran-4-one, as a novel
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to identify a flavonoid glycoside compound that may inhibit PCSK9, a protein that regulates cholesterol levels. The compound shows potential as an oral small-molecule alternative to expensive injectable antibody therapies for managing high cholesterol.

papers.title: Evaluation of deep learning architectures for predicting ligand interactions with neurologically relevant GPCRs.
papers.abstract: G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) are therapeutic targets for over 30% of approved drugs, yet specific GPCR subtypes act as molecular initiating events in several neurotoxic adverse outcome pathways. Therefore, knowledge of GPCR-ligand interactions is critical for drug discovery and computational toxicology. However, accurate predictions of GPCR-ligand binding can be challenging due to receptor conformational flexibility, complex membrane environment, and lack of selectivity among ligands. Drug-target interaction (DTI) models that jointly encode protein and ligand representations offer a pro
papers.summary: This study evaluates various deep learning architectures for predicting how ligands interact with neurologically relevant GPCRs, a key challenge in drug discovery and toxicology. The authors compare models that jointly encode protein and ligand representations, aiming to improve accuracy despite issues like receptor flexibility and ligand selectivity. The findings provide guidance on which architectures perform best for this specific and important class of drug targets.

papers.title: EvoDiffMol: evolutionary diffusion framework for 3D molecular design with optimized properties.
papers.abstract: Designing molecules with specific target properties remains a fundamental challenge in computational chemistry. While existing approaches show promise, most rely on simplified representations like SMILES strings or 2D graphs that lack essential three-dimensional geometric information. We present EvoDiffMol, a computational framework that integrates evolutionary algorithms with three-dimensional diffusion models for property-driven molecular generation. The method operates through adaptive evolutionary optimization, where population-based selection guides the generation process toward desired p
papers.summary: EvoDiffMol combines evolutionary algorithms with 3D diffusion models to generate molecules with optimized properties, overcoming the limitations of methods that rely on simplified 2D representations. This hybrid approach uses population-based selection to guide molecular generation toward desired characteristics while preserving essential three-dimensional geometric information.

papers.title: VITAGRAPH: building a knowledge graph for biologically relevant learning tasks.
papers.abstract: The complexity of human biology poses ongoing challenges, driving global interdisciplinary research. Artificial intelligence has become a powerful tool in computational biology, where graph data structures model entities like protein-protein interaction (PPI) networks and gene functional networks. These networks support crucial tasks in network medicine, including gene-disease association prediction, drug repurposing, and polypharmacy side-effect analysis. Reliable machine learning predictions require high-quality data. We present VITAGRAPH, a comprehensive multi-purpose biological knowledge g
papers.summary: VITAGRAPH is a new comprehensive biological knowledge graph designed to support machine learning tasks in network medicine, such as predicting gene-disease associations and drug repurposing. It integrates diverse biological data to provide high-quality inputs for reliable AI predictions.

papers.title: Prioritising search for virtual screening via preliminary interpretable low-feature likelihood-based rankings of drug-target activity measures.
papers.abstract: Current AI-based Virtual Screening (VS) methods seek to manage ultra-large molecular libraries. To this end, they develop increasingly efficient heuristics to rank ligands by their predicted activity against a target protein. However, these methods remain computationally demanding due to the billion-scale compound libraries that must be evaluated without prior, informed guidance.
papers.summary: This paper presents a method to prioritize which compounds to screen in virtual drug discovery by using simple, interpretable features to generate preliminary rankings of drug-target activity. This approach reduces the computational burden of evaluating billion-scale libraries by providing informed guidance before more complex AI methods are applied. The key contribution is a low-feature likelihood-based ranking that is both efficient and interpretable.

papers.title: Recent Advances in Organofluoroprobes for Brain Imaging: Progress, Challenges, and Future Directions.
papers.abstract: The precise and early diagnosis of neurodegenerative diseases remains a major challenge because of their highly complex and multifactorial pathophysiology. However, in recent years, the potential of organofluoroprobesorganic molecules designed to function as fluorescent probes in bioimaginghas become increasingly prominent. These probes enable visualization of essential biomarkers, including amyloid-beta (Aβ), tau, reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (ROS/RNS), neurotransmitters, and abnormal metal ions, facilitating early detection and monitoring of diseases such as Alzheimer's and Parkins
papers.summary: This review discusses recent advances in organofluoroprobes, which are organic fluorescent molecules used for brain imaging, highlighting their potential for early diagnosis of neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's. These probes can visualize key biomarkers such as amyloid-beta, tau, and reactive species, but challenges remain in their clinical translation.

papers.title: The role of AI-assisted drug repurposing in neurological disorders: a systematic review of validation strategies, challenges and opportunities.
papers.abstract: Neurological disorders refer to a diverse group of conditions that affect the brain, peripheral nerves, and spinal cord and impair socioemotional, cognitive, motor, and sensory functions. Alzheimer's disease (AD), Multiple Sclerosis (MS), Parkinson's disease (PD), Huntington's disease (HD), and Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) are some of the well-known neurodegenerative diseases that affect millions of people worldwide. Despite the advanced technologies and nano-drug delivery systems, the success rate of developing drugs for neurological disorders is significantly low. Among several constr
papers.summary: This systematic review examines how AI is being used to repurpose existing drugs for neurological disorders like Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, and ALS. It evaluates the validation strategies, challenges, and opportunities in this approach, highlighting that despite advanced drug delivery systems, success rates remain low.

papers.title: AI-guided identification of natural CTSL inhibitors with therapeutic potential for renal injury.
papers.abstract: Cathepsin L (CTSL) is a prominent therapeutic target for kidney injury, yet clinically available CTSL inhibitors remain limited. Here, we developed an artificial intelligence (AI)-assisted discovery strategy to identify novel CTSL inhibitors from a natural products library. Through a robust deep learning model and molecular docking, we screened 200 molecules from natural products library for experimental validation. Active candidates were further analyzed by molecular dynamics simulations to characterize binding modes and key CTSL-ligand interaction networks, followed by evaluation of therapeu
papers.summary: Researchers developed an AI-assisted strategy combining deep learning and molecular docking to identify natural compounds that inhibit Cathepsin L, a therapeutic target for kidney injury. From a library of natural products, they screened 200 molecules and validated active candidates through molecular dynamics simulations, revealing promising leads for renal injury treatment.

papers.title: DRIVE: a comprehensive resource deciphering drug-induced transcriptomic and splicing response in cancer cell.
papers.abstract: Pharmacotherapy induces complex molecular reprogramming in cancer, driving transcriptome-wide alterations and widespread dysregulation of alternative splicing. Despite these profound changes, there remain limited resources characterizing drug-induced whole-transcriptomic responses in cancer. Furthermore, while aberrant splicing can generate immunogenic neoantigens, existing resources fail to systematically integrate drug perturbations, splicing dynamics, and neoantigen landscapes. To address this gap, the DRIVE database was constructed as a comprehensive resource detailing drug-induced transcr
papers.summary: The DRIVE database is a new resource that catalogs how drugs alter gene expression and splicing in cancer cells, filling a gap in existing tools by also linking these changes to potential neoantigen production. It systematically integrates drug perturbations with transcriptomic and splicing data to help researchers understand pharmacotherapy-induced molecular reprogramming.

papers.title: Evaluation of Human-Approved Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients Against Lactate Dehydrogenase from Theileria annulata.
papers.abstract: BackgroundTheileriosis, a tick-borne disease caused by Theileria annulata, leads to global economic losses in livestock, emphasizing an urgent need for the identification of new therapeutic agents with alternative mechanisms of action due to rising buparvaquone resistance combined with climate-driven spread. T. annulata lactate dehydrogenase (TaLDH) has been selected as a promising target in the current study. As approximately half of the FDA-approved veterinary drugs are shared with human medicine, integrating drug repurposing has gained importance as an emerging strategy for the management o
papers.summary: This study identifies human-approved drugs that may be repurposed to treat theileriosis in livestock by targeting lactate dehydrogenase from the parasite Theileria annulata. The work addresses the urgent need for new treatments due to rising drug resistance and climate-driven spread of the disease.

papers.title: Decoding cytokine interactions for psoriasis therapy through computational repurposing of antihypertensive drugs.
papers.abstract: Psoriasis is a chronic, immune-mediated skin disorder characterized by excessive inflammation and the overexpression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, including IL-6, IL-1β, IL-17 A, and IFN-γ. Despite the availability of targeted therapies, current treatments often entail high costs, limited accessibility, and adverse side effects. Drug repurposing-utilizing approved medications for novel indications-offers a cost-effective strategy to accelerate the development of alternative therapies with established safety profiles. Twelve FDA-approved antihypertensive drugs-including ACE inhibitors, angiote
papers.summary: This study computationally repurposes FDA-approved antihypertensive drugs for psoriasis therapy by analyzing their interactions with key inflammatory cytokines. The findings suggest that certain antihypertensives may modulate cytokine activity, offering a potential cost-effective alternative to current psoriasis treatments.

papers.title: Venom-Derived Enzyme Inhibitors as Anticancer Agents: Structure-Activity Relationships, Molecular Targets and Mechanistic Insights.
papers.abstract: Animal venoms represent an extraordinary, yet largely untapped, biochemical reservoir for oncological drug discovery. This review provides a comprehensive analysis of venom-derived enzyme inhibitors as emerging anticancer agents, emphasizing their chemical diversity, structure-activity relationships (SAR), molecular targets, and mechanistic pathways. Venom-derived peptides and proteins exhibit exceptional binding affinity and structural rigidity, characteristics frequently enforced by conserved disulfide networks. This specific architecture allows them to selectively modulate critical cancer-a
papers.summary: This review explores how animal venoms, which contain diverse peptides and proteins with high binding affinity and structural stability, can be repurposed as enzyme inhibitors for cancer treatment. It focuses on their chemical diversity, structure-activity relationships, and mechanisms of action against cancer targets.

papers.title: AD-LSF: A Multimodal Framework for Drug-Target Interaction Prediction Based on Asymmetric Dynamic Gated Modulation and Adaptive Latent Signal Fusion.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of drug-target interactions (DTIs) is fundamental to drug discovery and repurposing. Although deep learning-based multimodal integration has advanced DTI modeling, current approaches remain limited in capturing complex cross-modal nonlinear dependencies and in reconciling distributional heterogeneity across data modalities. To address these limitations, we propose a multimodal DTI prediction framework termed Asymmetric Dynamic Gated Modulation and Adaptive Latent Signal Fusion (AD-LSF), which is built upon asymmetric dynamic gating modulation and adaptive latent signal fusi
papers.summary: This paper introduces AD-LSF, a new deep learning framework for predicting drug-target interactions that improves upon existing methods by better capturing complex relationships between different data types (modalities) and handling differences in their statistical distributions. The model uses asymmetric dynamic gating and adaptive signal fusion to integrate multimodal drug and target data, potentially improving drug discovery and repurposing efforts.

papers.title: Design and computational evaluation of E-stilbene-bearing 1,3,4-oxadiazole derivatives as potential tyrosinase inhibitors using DFT, molecular docking and ADMET studies.
papers.abstract: A series of E-stilbene-bearing 1,3,4-oxadiazole derivatives (4a-b and 5a-f) was synthesized via the Mizoroki-Heck reaction and evaluated as inhibitors of tyrosinase, a vital enzyme involved in melanin biosynthesis. The synthesized compounds were structurally confirmed through NMR spectroscopy (1H NMR, 13C NMR), infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and mass spectrometry. Nearly all derivatives exhibited inhibitory effects against tyrosinase with IC50 values ranging from 0.32 ± 0.03 µM to 6.28 ± 0.05 µM. Among them, compounds 5d and 5b illustrated the most significant inhibitory activity, with IC50 va
papers.summary: This study synthesized and tested new E-stilbene-based compounds as tyrosinase inhibitors, finding several with strong activity (IC50 as low as 0.32 µM). The best candidates were validated using computational methods including DFT, molecular docking, and ADMET analysis, suggesting they could be developed further for skin-lightening or anti-browning applications.

papers.title: Therapeutic potential of Citrus medica L. (cv. 'Liscia' and cv. 'Rugosa') phytocompounds targeting biofilm formation, quorum sensing, and antioxidant defense mechanisms.
papers.abstract: Microbial biofilm, quorum sensing (QS)-mediated virulence and oxidative stress-induced inflammation are interrelated pathological processes that play a crucial role in persistent infections and chronic illnesses. Due to the multifactorially of such processes, isolated target-based approaches to therapy usually do not provide long-term effectiveness. A combined network pharmacology and multi-level computational approach was used in order to clarify the molecular mechanisms underlying the antibiofilm, anti-quorum sensing (anti-QS), and antioxidant activities of thirty-three bioactive compounds f
papers.summary: This study uses network pharmacology and computational modeling to investigate how compounds from two Citrus medica varieties (Liscia and Rugosa) might simultaneously target biofilm formation, quorum sensing, and oxidative stress. The findings suggest these phytocompounds could serve as multi-target therapeutic agents for persistent infections and chronic inflammatory diseases.

papers.title: Network pharmacology and in silico analysis of Azadirachta indica phytoconstituents reveal potential hepatoprotective targets and mechanisms.
papers.abstract: Network pharmacology and bioinformatics approaches may provide valuable insights into pharmacological effects by enabling a system-level understanding of how drugs interact with biological networks rather than single targets. The study aimed to elucidate the therapeutic mechanisms of Azadirachta indica leaf extract (AILE) in hepatotoxicity and hepato-injury (HT/HI) through the identification of pathways and molecular targets via network pharmacology. Phytochemical profiling of AILE was performed by Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (GC-MS). SMILES structures of identified phytochemicals wer
papers.summary: This study uses network pharmacology and bioinformatics to identify potential hepatoprotective mechanisms of neem (Azadirachta indica) leaf extract. By analyzing phytochemicals via GC-MS and computational target prediction, the authors map out key pathways and molecular targets that may underlie the extract's protective effects against liver injury.

papers.title: From Chatbots to Co-Scientists: The Impact of Knowledge-Generating AI (AI 4.0) on Healthcare and Research.
papers.abstract: While artificial intelligence (AI) has developed as a computational concept for 70 years, its transformative impact on biomedical research and healthcare has explosively accelerated over just the last five years. We are now standing at the precipice of a profound paradigm shift: The emergence of fourth-generation, knowledge-generating AI (AI 4.0). Unlike its predecessors, which primarily synthesized existing information, AI 4.0 possesses advanced reasoning and multi-agentic capabilities that elevate it from a passive tool to an autonomous "co-scientist." By independently formulating novel, tes
papers.summary: This perspective article argues that a new generation of AI (AI 4.0) with advanced reasoning and multi-agent capabilities is emerging, transforming AI from a passive tool into an autonomous 'co-scientist' capable of independently generating novel, testable hypotheses. The authors discuss how this paradigm shift is poised to fundamentally change biomedical research and healthcare by accelerating discovery and enabling new forms of scientific collaboration.

papers.title: A benchmark dataset and interpretable deep learning framework for drug-induced developmental neurotoxicity prediction.
papers.abstract: Developmental neurotoxicity (DNT) represents a critical yet underevaluated toxicity endpoint within current chemical safety assessment frameworks, particularly in the context of drug development. In the present study, we constructed a standardized benchmark dataset comprising 2724 structurally curated compounds, including 1362 DNT-positive chemicals derived from the Developmental Neurotox Reference List (DNTREF) of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and 1362 property-matched presumed negative compounds (non-neuroactive drug-like compounds) selected from the ChEMBL database. Using t
papers.summary: The authors created a benchmark dataset of 2,724 compounds for predicting developmental neurotoxicity (DNT) and developed an interpretable deep learning framework to classify DNT-positive versus negative chemicals. The dataset combines EPA DNT reference chemicals with matched non-neuroactive compounds from ChEMBL, enabling more reliable model training and evaluation.

papers.title: Unveiling acetamide coupled benzothiazole hydrazones as potential anti-proliferative agents: an experimental and computational approach.
papers.abstract: A series of benzothiazole acetamide hydrazones (8a-p) was synthesized and evaluated for anti-proliferative activity against various human cancer cell lines, including a pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (CAPAN-1), a colorectal carcinoma (HCT-116), a leukemia (HL60), a glioblastoma (LN229), a T lymphoblast (Molt-4), a non-small cell lung cancer (NCI-H460), and a B lymphoblast (Z138) cell line. Among the tested derivatives, compounds 8l, 8n, and 8o emerged as lead compounds, exhibiting cytotoxicity across multiple cell lines. Notably, compound 8o demonstrated selective cytotoxicity towards Z138 a
papers.summary: Researchers synthesized and tested a series of benzothiazole-based compounds against seven human cancer cell lines, identifying three lead compounds (8l, 8n, 8o) with broad anti-proliferative activity. One compound (8o) showed selective toxicity toward a B lymphoblast cell line, suggesting potential for targeted cancer therapy.

papers.title: Multiscale systems modelling of communication networks in brain metastasis.
papers.abstract: Brain metastasis remains a major clinical challenge because intracranial progression and therapeutic resistance arise not only from tumour-intrinsic programmes but also from coordinated communication across molecular, cellular, spatial and systemic scales. In this Review, we integrate findings from spatial transcriptomics/proteomics, single-cell and multimodal omics, and computational systems modelling to frame brain metastasis as a multiscale communication disorder in which immune, glial, vascular and systemic networks collectively shape tumour-permissive ecosystems. We discuss how network-ba
papers.summary: This review integrates spatial transcriptomics, single-cell omics, and computational modeling to frame brain metastasis as a multiscale communication disorder involving immune, glial, vascular, and systemic networks. It highlights how network-based analyses can reveal therapeutic vulnerabilities beyond tumor-intrinsic programs.

papers.title: Withanolide A inhibits hIAPP aggregation: An In silico, biophysical, and drosophila-based In vivo validation.
papers.abstract: The aberrant aggregation of human islet amyloid polypeptide (hIAPP) or Amylin into toxic oligomers and fibrils leads to pancreatic β-cell dysfunction and progressive cell death, which is a key pathological feature of Type II diabetes mellitus (T2DM). In this study, we adopted a hybrid approach combining virtual screening and molecular dynamics (MD) simulation, with experimental validation, to identify inhibitors of hIAPP aggregation. Herein, we screened 2000 phytoconstituents from natural products using molecular docking, followed by in silico ADMET predictions. Withaferin A and Withanolide A
papers.summary: This study identifies Withanolide A, a natural compound, as an inhibitor of hIAPP aggregation, which is linked to pancreatic β-cell death in Type 2 diabetes. Using computational screening, molecular dynamics, and experimental validation in fruit flies, the authors show that Withanolide A reduces toxic amyloid aggregation. The work highlights a potential therapeutic lead from natural products for diabetes treatment.

papers.title: Topological deep learning for drug-target interaction, virtual screening, and docking scoring: a practical, benchmark-driven review.
papers.abstract: Artificial intelligence is now central to computational drug discovery, yet performance in core tasks-drug-target interaction (DTI) prediction, virtual screening (VS), and docking scoring-is still limited by the multiscale geometric nature of molecular recognition and by evaluation pitfalls such as dataset bias and leakage. Topological deep learning (TDL) offers a complementary route to encode global and multiscale structure from ligands, binding pockets, surfaces, and protein-ligand complexes via persistent homology and related constructions. This review provides a practical, task-driven synt
papers.summary: This review examines how topological deep learning (TDL), which uses persistent homology to capture multiscale geometric features of molecules, can improve drug-target interaction prediction, virtual screening, and docking scoring. The authors highlight that TDL offers a complementary approach to address limitations of current AI methods, such as dataset bias and leakage, but emphasize the need for rigorous benchmarking to realize its potential.

papers.title: Targeting HsDHODH: Shape and machine-learning guided discovery and structural validation of SARS-CoV-2 antivirals.
papers.abstract: The frequent emergence of novel RNA viruses and the rapid development of viral resistance highlight the urgent need for new antiviral therapies. In this study, we employed an integrated computational pipeline combining shape-based models, machine-learning (ML)-based models, and hotspot analysis to identify novel human dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (HsDHODH) inhibitors. A virtual screening (VS) campaign of the H3D chemical library identified H3D-002856 as the primary hit (IC50 = 2.92 ± 0.07 μM), which subsequently guided a similarity-based hit expansion (Tanimoto coefficient≥0.7) of 17 structura
papers.summary: Researchers used a combination of shape-based modeling, machine learning, and hotspot analysis to discover new inhibitors of the human enzyme HsDHODH as potential antivirals against SARS-CoV-2. Virtual screening identified a lead compound, H3D-002856, with micromolar activity, which was then used to guide the discovery of 17 additional structurally related compounds.

papers.title: Advancing bioinformatics with language models: components, applications, and perspectives.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are deep learning-based artificial intelligence models that have achieved remarkable success in natural language processing. Typically composed of neural networks with billions of parameters, they are trained on massive unlabeled datasets using self-supervised or semi-supervised learning. Beyond language, LLMs hold immense potential for addressing complex bioinformatics challenges. This review provides a comprehensive overview of transformer-based model applications in genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, drug discovery, and single-cell analysis. We discuss criti
papers.summary: This review provides a comprehensive overview of how large language models (LLMs), particularly transformer-based architectures, are being applied across bioinformatics domains including genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, drug discovery, and single-cell analysis. It discusses the components and training paradigms of LLMs and highlights their potential to address complex biological challenges beyond natural language processing.

papers.title: Investigating the anticancer activity of eravacycline in pancreatic cancer via target-based deep learning and experimental validation.
papers.abstract: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is a highly lethal malignancy with limited therapeutic options. In this study, we introduce a target-based deep learning framework to investigate the anticancer activity of eravacycline (Erav), a United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved antibacterial agent previously identified in our work as a potential anticancer candidate through computational screening. We developed a novel two-phase in silico yeast-based prediction model to explore potential mechanisms of action, followed by in vitro and in vivo experimental validation. DNA polymera
papers.summary: This study uses a deep learning framework to predict and validate that the FDA-approved antibiotic eravacycline has anticancer activity against pancreatic cancer. The model identified DNA polymerase as a potential target, which was confirmed through in vitro and in vivo experiments.

papers.title: Amide-functionalized cholic acid hybrids: design, bioactivity, and computational analysis.
papers.abstract: A series of amide-linked cholic acid derivatives (3a-f) was synthesized through an efficient one-pot acyl chloride coupling strategy, yielding structurally diverse hybrids under mild conditions. The antioxidant potential of these derivatives was evaluated using the DPPH radical scavenging assay, with compounds 3b-d exhibiting the most pronounced activity, in an average of 50%. Antibacterial efficacy was investigated against both Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli, revealing preferential activity toward Gram-positive strains, particularly for aromatic amide-bearing analogs. To elucidate
papers.summary: This study reports the synthesis of new amide-linked cholic acid derivatives and tests their antioxidant and antibacterial activities. Some derivatives showed moderate antioxidant effects and selective activity against Gram-positive bacteria, with computational analysis used to understand the results.

papers.title: Integrating machine learning and structure-based simulations to prioritise Z4P as a mutation-resilient IRE1α inhibitor for breast cancer.
papers.abstract: Breast cancer is one of the most prevalent and lethal malignancies affecting women globally. The increasing resistance to current therapeutic strategies highlights the need for novel molecular targets. Inositol-requiring enzyme 1 alpha (IRE1α), a key sensor in the unfolded protein response (UPR), has emerged as a promising therapeutic target due to its role in tumour progression and survival. This study employed an integrative in silico approach combining machine learning, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulations to identify potent, non-toxic IRE1α inhibitors for breast cancer tre
papers.summary: This study used machine learning and molecular simulations to identify Z4P as a promising IRE1α inhibitor for breast cancer that may be resilient to mutations. The compound showed stable binding in computational tests and could represent a new therapeutic option for drug-resistant breast cancer.

papers.title: Revolutionizing drug discovery from natural products: The roles of artificial intelligence and multi-omics in accelerating innovation.
papers.abstract: Natural products and their derivatives have long been crucial in drug therapy, especially in traditional medicine. However, challenges in screening, isolation, characterization, and optimization have slowed their development in the pharmaceutical industry. Recent advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) and multi-omics technologies are revitalizing this field. AI offers powerful tools for understanding natural compounds, enhancing molecular representations, and supporting tasks such as binding prediction, drug repurposing, and retrosynthesis. Moreover, generative models are aiding in natur
papers.summary: This review discusses how artificial intelligence and multi-omics technologies are overcoming traditional bottlenecks in natural product drug discovery, including screening, isolation, and optimization. AI enhances molecular representation, binding prediction, drug repurposing, and retrosynthesis, while generative models support natural product-based drug design. The paper highlights a revitalization of natural product research through computational innovation.

papers.title: AI-powered therapeutic aptamer drug discovery: Targeting the CT-domain of CTGF for duchenne muscular dystrophy.
papers.abstract: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD), a fatal X-linked disorder, features progressive muscle fibrosis as a key driver of mortality. While CTGF represents a therapeutic target for DMD, its VWC-domain-targeting antibody (FG-3019) failed in clinical trials. Through experimental validation, we identified CT-domain as a superior target domain, as it contributed more fibrosis activity of CTGF than VWC-domain without elevating compensatory TGF-β1 level. Aptamers are synthetic oligonucleotides identified through SELEX, which can specifically bind to flexible protein domains through their unique 3D confor
papers.summary: This study identifies the CT-domain of CTGF as a superior therapeutic target for Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) compared to the VWC-domain, which was targeted by a failed antibody. Using AI-powered aptamer discovery, the authors developed aptamers that bind the CT-domain, reducing fibrosis without elevating compensatory TGF-β1 levels. This work provides a new therapeutic strategy for DMD by targeting a more disease-relevant protein domain.

papers.title: FlowDock: A unified flow-based framework for flexible protein-ligand docking and binding affinity prediction.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of protein-ligand complexes and binding affinity is critical for hit identification and optimization for structure-based drug design. Traditional docking simulates binding processes with searching algorithms guided by energy-scoring functions, which are quite computationally expensive and time-intensive. In contrast, deep learning approaches offer a cost-effective alternative, yet often generate conformations with limited physicochemical validity and fail to account for protein flexibility. To address these pitfalls, we propose FlowDock, a multitask framework enhanced by Ba
papers.summary: FlowDock is a new deep learning framework that simultaneously predicts protein-ligand binding poses and binding affinity, addressing key limitations of traditional docking (slow speed) and prior AI methods (poor physical validity and lack of protein flexibility). By using a flow-based model, it generates more realistic protein-ligand complexes efficiently, offering a promising tool for faster and more accurate drug discovery.

papers.title: Editor Profiles: Guest Editors of the Special Column on Machine Learning in Drug Discovery.
papers.summary: This editorial introduces guest editors for a special column on machine learning in drug discovery, highlighting the growing role of computational methods in pharmaceutical research. It provides context for the collection of papers but does not present new scientific findings.

papers.title: Artificial intelligence in biologic drug discovery: A review of methodological evolution and therapeutic applications.
papers.abstract: Biologic drugs, primarily comprising proteins and nucleic acids, have emerged as powerful therapeutic modalities; however, their discovery and optimization are often hindered by their inherent complexity. The advent of artificial intelligence (AI), particularly deep learning, is catalyzing a paradigm shift in this field, transitioning it from a process reliant on serendipity and laborious experimentation to a data-driven engineering discipline. This review systematically charts the co-evolution of AI methodologies and their transformative applications across the modern biologic drug developmen
papers.summary: This review examines how artificial intelligence, especially deep learning, is transforming biologic drug discovery from a trial-and-error process into a data-driven engineering discipline. It systematically maps the co-evolution of AI methods and their applications in developing protein and nucleic acid therapeutics.

papers.title: Editorial of special column on machine learning in drug discovery.
papers.summary: This editorial introduces a special column on machine learning in drug discovery, highlighting recent advances and applications of AI/ML methods in pharmaceutical research. It provides an overview of how computational approaches are accelerating drug development processes.

papers.title: LEARNER: A Transfer Learning Method for Low-Rank Matrix Estimation.
papers.abstract: Low-rank matrix estimation is a fundamental problem in statistics and machine learning with applications across biomedical sciences, including genetics, medical imaging, drug discovery, and electronic health record data analysis. In the context of heterogeneous data generated from diverse sources, a key challenge lies in leveraging data from a source population to enhance the estimation of a low-rank matrix in a target population of interest. We propose an approach that leverages similarity in the latent row and column spaces between the source and target populations to improve estimation in t
papers.summary: This paper introduces LEARNER, a transfer learning method that improves low-rank matrix estimation in a target population by leveraging shared latent row and column structures from a source population. The approach is particularly relevant for biomedical applications like genetics and drug discovery where data from multiple sources can be combined to enhance estimation accuracy.

papers.title: Artificial Intelligence in Pharmaceutical Care: Transforming Drug Discovery, Development, and Patient-care Delivery.
papers.abstract: The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into pharmaceutical care represents a paradigm shift in healthcare delivery, offering unprecedented opportunities to revolutionize medication management, accelerate drug development, and enhance patient outcomes. This comprehensive review synthesized evidence from global literature (2021-2025) examining AI applications across drug discovery and development, clinical trials optimization, medication therapy management, and pharmacy operations. The AI demonstrated prediction accuracies of 86-98% in drug discovery, 80% improvement in clinical trial r
papers.summary: This review synthesizes recent literature on AI applications in pharmaceutical care, finding that AI achieves 86-98% accuracy in drug discovery and 80% improvement in clinical trial processes. The paper provides a broad overview of how AI is transforming medication management, drug development, and patient care delivery.

papers.title: Exploring drug repurposing for monkeypox virus: A structural and computational approach.
papers.abstract: The increasing global threat posed by viral infections that are transmitted to humans from animals has intensified interest in emerging pathogens such as monkeypox virus (MPV). This study investigated the potential of drug repurposing as an efficient therapeutic strategy against MPV, focusing on its close genetic relationships with vaccinia and variola viruses.
papers.journal: Journal of Taibah University Medical Sciences
papers.summary: This study explores drug repurposing as a strategy to treat monkeypox virus by leveraging its genetic similarity to vaccinia and variola viruses. Using structural and computational methods, the authors identify existing drugs that may be effective against MPV, offering a faster path to treatment than developing new antivirals.

papers.title: AI-driven drug discovery using transformer-based molecular representation learning.
papers.abstract: The vast majority of chemically plausible, drug-like molecules remain unexplored due to the combinatorial scale of chemical space and the limited throughput of experimental screening. This is complicated by the lack of data and the inability to extrapolate predictive models to chemotypes that are not represented well. We introduce a transformer-based molecular modeling framework for target-specific potency prediction, trained on curated BindingDB bioactivity data across Alzheimer's, diabetes, and cancer targets to deliver accurate pIC50 regression and binary activity classification. It uses cu
papers.summary: This paper presents a transformer-based AI framework that predicts drug potency (pIC50) and classifies activity for specific targets, trained on curated bioactivity data for Alzheimer's, diabetes, and cancer. The model addresses the challenge of exploring vast chemical space by improving prediction accuracy for underrepresented chemotypes, offering a computational tool to accelerate early-stage drug discovery.

papers.title: Recent applications of artificial intelligence in cancer radiotherapy and immunotherapy: current status and future directions.
papers.abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) enhances the precision, personalization, and efficiency of cancer treatment through deep learning and machine learning techniques. This review comprehensively examines the evolution of AI and its expanding applications in cancer radiotherapy, immunotherapy, and drug discovery and repurposing. In radiotherapy, AI enables automated medical image segmentation, thereby facilitating the accurate delineation of tumor targets. Furthermore, AI-driven feedback systems support clinicians in developing individualized treatment plans by offering real-time assessment of treatme
papers.summary: This review article discusses how artificial intelligence, particularly deep learning and machine learning, is improving cancer treatment by enhancing precision in radiotherapy through automated image segmentation and enabling personalized treatment plans with real-time feedback. The authors also cover AI applications in immunotherapy and drug discovery, providing a comprehensive overview of current uses and future directions in oncology.

papers.title: Calibrated Weighted Rank Aggregation for Virtual Screening Independently Rediscovers Privileged Vitamin D Receptor Ligand Scaffolds.
papers.abstract: Computational drug discovery relies on virtual screening to prioritize a small number of compounds for experimental testing, yet combining heterogeneous in silico scoring methods into a single robust ranking remains challenging. We present Calibrated Weighted Rank Aggregation (CWRA), a data-driven rank-optimized score-fusion framework that (a) direction-corrects modality outputs, (b) harmonizes them via per-modality normalization, and (c) learns nonnegative fusion weights from known actives by optimizing a rank-based early-recognition objective (Boltzmann-enhanced discrimination of receiver op
papers.summary: This paper introduces Calibrated Weighted Rank Aggregation (CWRA), a new computational method that combines different virtual screening scores into a single, robust ranking to improve drug discovery. The authors show that CWRA independently rediscovers known privileged scaffolds for the Vitamin D receptor, demonstrating its ability to prioritize active compounds without prior knowledge of these scaffolds. This approach offers a principled way to fuse heterogeneous scoring methods, potentially improving the efficiency of computational drug discovery.

papers.title: T-pGNN4DTI: Towards better drug-target interactions prediction using Global Self-attentive Pooled Graph Convolutional Networks and protein pre-training Models.
papers.abstract: Identification of drug-target interactions (DTI) is an important and challenging task in drug discovery and development. Traditional methods generally require biological experiments, which are costly and time-consuming. Machine learning-based methods can rapidly predict DTI using only computer algorithmic models, allowing researchers to validate only the most promising interactions through biochemical experiments. This holds promise for effectively addressing the current challenges of lengthy development cycles and high costs in new drug development. However, it is difficult for the existing D
papers.summary: This paper presents T-pGNN4DTI, a new machine learning method that combines graph neural networks with protein pre-training models to predict drug-target interactions more accurately. The approach uses a global self-attentive pooling mechanism to better capture important features from both drug and protein structures, potentially reducing the need for costly experimental validation in drug discovery.

papers.title: International perspectives on AI-driven pharmaceutical IP challenges.
papers.abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming the pharmaceutical sector by optimizing care delivery and accelerating drug design. While AI introduces significant benefits, it presents complex challenges regarding intellectual property (IP) frameworks. This review examines AI-driven innovations in personalized medicine and drug design, with a specific focus on inventorship issues that directly affect pharmaceutical patent protection. We analyze recent United States (US) legal developments concerning AI inventorship, including the US Supreme Court's March 2026 denial of certiorari in Thaler v. Pe
papers.summary: This review examines how AI is reshaping intellectual property challenges in the pharmaceutical industry, particularly around inventorship for patents. It analyzes recent US legal developments, including the Supreme Court's 2026 decision in Thaler v. Perlmutter, to highlight the tension between AI-driven innovation and existing IP frameworks.

papers.title: GraphTransDTI: A novel hybrid framework combining graph transformer and CNN-BiLSTM for enhanced Drug-Protein Interaction prediction.
papers.abstract: Drug-protein interaction (DTI) prediction is a pivotal step in the drug discovery and repurposing process, helping to minimize experimental costs and time. However, existing deep learning methods often face limitations in simultaneously capturing the spatial structure of drug molecules and the deep contextual correlation with protein sequences. To address this issue, we propose GraphTransDTI, a synergistic hybrid framework that integrates a Graph Transformer to represent drug graph structures, a CNN-BiLSTM network to encode protein sequence context, and a Cross-Attention mechanism to model cro
papers.summary: GraphTransDTI is a new deep learning method that combines a Graph Transformer for drug molecular structures with a CNN-BiLSTM network for protein sequences, using cross-attention to better predict drug-protein interactions. It outperforms existing models on benchmark datasets, offering a more accurate and efficient approach for drug discovery and repurposing.

papers.title: AI-driven drug reposition for pathogens: a new paradigm in pandemic preparedness.
papers.abstract: Faced with a severe outbreak of diseases caused by newly emerging and recurrent pathogens, the development cycle of traditional drugs is long, making it difficult to meet emergency needs. Drug repositioning has become a key strategy for rapidly providing therapies by exploring new therapeutic uses of approved drugs. However, traditional reposition methods have bottlenecks such as slow speed and strong randomness. Artificial intelligence (AI) is revolutionizing drug reposition by analyzing and integrating multi-source data with computational models, dramatically accelerating the discovery proce
papers.summary: This paper reviews how artificial intelligence can accelerate drug repositioning—finding new uses for existing drugs—to rapidly respond to emerging infectious disease outbreaks. AI overcomes limitations of traditional methods by quickly analyzing diverse data sources, making it a promising strategy for pandemic preparedness.

papers.title: Computational and experimental evaluation of Pisolithus arhizus metabolites targeting major efflux pumps of mastitis-associated Staphylococcus aureus.
papers.abstract: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in pathogenic bacteria, particularly Staphylococcus aureus, is an increasing global concern in veterinary medicine. The present study evaluated bioactive metabolites from P. arhizus as potential inhibitors of major S. aureus efflux pumps. The methanolic extracts of P. arhizus were profiled using GC-MS to identify the main constituents, and their antibacterial activity against S. aureus was evaluated using a well diffusion assay. The experimental results showed that the crude methanolic extract exhibited a 20 mm zone of inhibition with a MIC of 30 µg/mL. Furthermo
papers.summary: This study identifies compounds from the fungus Pisolithus arhizus that can inhibit efflux pumps in drug-resistant Staphylococcus aureus, a major cause of mastitis in livestock. Using GC-MS profiling and lab experiments, the crude extract showed strong antibacterial activity, suggesting a potential natural alternative for treating resistant infections.

papers.title: Targeting PKLR and lipogenic enzymes through JNK inhibition to develop a therapeutic strategy for MASLD and MASH.
papers.abstract: Pyruvate kinase liver and red blood cells (PKLR) is linked to metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD). Previous study, we identified JNK-IN-5A, a c-Jun N-terminal kinase (JNK) inhibitor that suppresses PKL expression in HepG2 cells using computational drug repurposing and screened out four hit JNK-IN-5A derivatives (SET-151, SET-152, SET-162, SET-130).
papers.summary: This study identifies that inhibiting the JNK pathway with the compound JNK-IN-5A and its derivatives suppresses PKLR and lipogenic enzyme expression, offering a potential therapeutic strategy for MASLD and MASH. The work builds on computational drug repurposing to find compounds that reduce fat accumulation in liver cells.

papers.title: Tissue-autonomous pharmacological direction: how target expression landscapes convert balanced compounds into tissue-selective agents.
papers.abstract: Multi-target drugs frequently exhibit tissue-dependent pharmacological directions: the same compound may promote a biological process in one organ while driving its functional opposite in another. Tamoxifen antagonizes estrogen receptors in breast tissue yet activates them in the endometrium; non-selective NSAIDs reduce inflammation at injury sites while damaging the gastric mucosa. Each phenomenon has been explained by drug-specific mechanisms, but no unifying principle predicts when or why such direction reversal occurs. Here we propose tissue-autonomous pharmacological direction (TAPD): whe
papers.summary: This paper proposes a unifying principle called tissue-autonomous pharmacological direction (TAPD) to explain why multi-target drugs can have opposite effects in different tissues, such as tamoxifen acting as an antagonist in breast tissue but an agonist in the endometrium. The authors suggest that the expression landscape of drug targets across tissues determines whether a balanced compound will produce tissue-selective effects, providing a framework to predict and understand these paradoxical drug behaviors.

papers.title: Computational evaluation of selected compounds as potential inhibitors of gastric H⁺/K⁺-ATPase using molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, MMGBSA, and ADMET analyses.
papers.abstract: This study investigated the inhibitory potential of selected phytochemicals and conventional drugs against gastric H⁺/K⁺-ATPase, an important therapeutic target associated with acid-related gastrointestinal disorders such as peptic ulcers and gastroesophageal reflux disease. Computational approaches, including molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, MMGBSA free energy calculations, and ADMET profiling, were employed to evaluate the pharmacological relevance of the selected compounds. Molecular docking analysis revealed that several compounds exhibited favorable interactions with the
papers.summary: This study used computational methods to evaluate how well certain plant compounds and drugs might inhibit gastric H⁺/K⁺-ATPase, a key target for treating acid-related stomach disorders. Molecular docking showed that some compounds had promising interactions with the enzyme, suggesting they could be potential new treatments.

papers.title: Computational QSAR and structure-based identification of plerixafor-derived PIM-1 kinase inhibitors in diffuse large B-Cell lymphoma.
papers.abstract: Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) is the most common and aggressive subtype of non-Hodgkin lymphoma, with a substantial proportion of patients developing resistance to standard chemotherapy. Chromosomal translocations resulting in overexpression of the serine/threonine kinase Proviral Integration site for Moloney murine leukemia virus (PIM-1) contribute to disease progression, therapeutic resistance, and poor clinical outcomes, establishing PIM-1 as a promising molecular target. Unlike many kinases, the ATP-binding site of PIM-1 lacks a backbone hydrogen bond donor within the hinge region
papers.summary: This study used computational methods to identify derivatives of the drug plerixafor as potential inhibitors of the PIM-1 kinase, a target in diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. The researchers found that these compounds could bind to PIM-1's unique ATP-binding site, offering a new approach for treating chemotherapy-resistant lymphoma.

papers.title: Computer-aided drug discovery: historical foundations, practical AI tools, and emerging ethical considerations.
papers.abstract: Computer-aided drug discovery (CADD) has become an integral component of modern drug development, supporting hit identification, lead optimisation, and candidate refinement across both academia and industry. Over 4 decades of methodological progress have contributed to the discovery or optimisation of several approved therapeutics, establishing CADD as a crucial element of drug research. In parallel, recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) have introduced a new generation of practical tools that offer improved predictive performance, accessible software implem
papers.summary: This review covers the historical development of computer-aided drug discovery (CADD) and highlights how recent AI/ML tools are improving predictive performance and accessibility. It also discusses emerging ethical considerations in the field, providing a comprehensive overview for researchers and practitioners.

papers.title: Uncovering antioxidant and anti-breast cancer potential metabolite from streptomyces strain sp. VITAMB using integrated experimental and computational approaches.
papers.abstract: Actinobacteria's are rich in bioactive compounds, showing antimicrobial, antiviral, antioxidative, anti-inflammatory, and anticancer activity. The increasing demand for these metabolites in the culinary, pharmaceutical, cosmetic, and nutraceutical industries emphasizes the latent potential of actinomycetes. Paddy field ecosystem provides unique physicochemical conditions that favor the growth of actinomycetes, making them a valuable reservoir of pigment-producing microorganisms. In the present study, a yellow pigment-producing strain identified as Streptomyces sp (GenBank ID: OR958670) was iso
papers.summary: This study identifies a yellow pigment-producing Streptomyces strain from paddy soil that produces metabolites with antioxidant and anti-breast cancer activity, characterized through both experimental assays and computational modeling. The findings highlight the potential of soil actinomycetes as a source of bioactive compounds for pharmaceutical applications.

papers.title: MetaphorPrompt2-A Structure and Function-Focused Approach for Extracting Causal Events from Biological Text.
papers.abstract: Extracting molecular regulatory pathways (MRPs) from biomedical literature is crucial for building knowledge graphs that represent disease mechanisms and molecular regulation. However, even advanced large language models (LLMs) using in-context learning often misinterpret complex domain-specific causal statements or omit intermediary steps, resulting in incomplete pathway representations. MetaphorPrompt2 is motivated by cognitive theories of structural mapping and causal event representation. It improves MRP extraction by emphasizing structural relations and functional roles of biological enti
papers.summary: MetaphorPrompt2 is a new method that improves how large language models extract molecular regulatory pathways from biomedical text by focusing on structural relationships and functional roles of biological entities, rather than just keywords. This approach helps capture causal events and intermediary steps that other methods often miss, leading to more complete pathway representations for building knowledge graphs of disease mechanisms.

papers.title: GDA-Pred: Generative AI-Driven Data Augmentation for Improved Prediction of IL-6 and IL-13 Inducing Peptides
papers.abstract: The identification of interleukin-6 (IL-6) and interleukin-13 (IL-13) inducing peptides is crucial for accelerating drug discovery targeting cancer, immune disorders, and infectious diseases. However, experimental screening methods remain time-consuming and costly. To address these limitations, various machine learning and deep learning models have been developed, yet their performance is still constrained by the limited availability of experimentally validated data. In this study, we propose a generative AI-driven data augmentation (GDA) framework and predictor, GDA-Pred, to improve the predi
papers.summary: This paper introduces GDA-Pred, a framework that uses generative AI to create synthetic training data for better prediction of peptides that induce IL-6 and IL-13, which are important targets for drug discovery. By augmenting limited experimental data, the approach improves the performance of machine learning models for identifying these peptides.
